coredata.dc:title: Carbon nitride quantum dots control RuO2-TiO2/Ti coated anodes for degradation of high concentration phenol
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Zhengyi
Han, Zhaohui
Xu, Yang
Wei, Sikang
Xu, Lei
Zhou, Shenggang
coredata.dc:description: In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were fabricated by a one-step hydrothermal method, and RuO2-TiO2-N-CQDs/Ti composite-coated anodes were fabricated using a sol-gel method. The results indicate that the doping of N-CQDs generates many vacancy defects on the surface of the composite coating. As the content of N-CQDs increases, the width and depth of surface cracks on the coating gradually decrease, and the crystal size decreases. The doping of N-CQDs can effectively improve the electrochemical performance of the anode. When the doping amount of N-CQDs is 9.7 %, the surface charge capacity is increased by 243.5 %, the polarisation potential is decreased by 160 mV, and the self-corrosion potential is increased by 87.1 %. Compared to the traditional RuO2-TiO2/Ti anode, when the content of N-CQDs is increased to 9.7 %, the removal efficiency of phenol on the RuO2-TiO2-N-CQDs/Ti composite-coated electrode is increased by 93.2 %. In comparison, the removal efficiency of chemical oxygen demand within 1 h is increased by 48.7 %. In this study, we fully utilised the effective electron transfer and structural changes induced by N element doping to improve the electrocatalytic performance of the electrode, providing a new approach for mineralising high-concentration phenol in Ru/Ti electrodes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
RuO2-TiO2-Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots/Ti electrode
Electrocatalytic oxidation technology
Phenol degradation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis pathway of the RuO2-TiO2-N-CQDs/Ti anode.
(a) FTIR spectroscopy, (b–e) XPS, (f) TEM, particle diameter distribution, and (g–k) element mapping of N-CQDs.
(a–e) TEM, (f–g) SEM, (h) XRD, and (i–k) XPS of RuO2-TiO2-N-CQDs/Ti anodes.

RuO2-TiO2-N-CQDs/Ti electrodes with different N-CQDs contents: (a) EIS, (b) CV, (c) LSV, and (d) Tafel curves. (e) Phenol degradation rate, (f) COD removal rate, (g) CV curves for different cycles, and (h) charge capacity, polarisation potential, and self-corrosion potential.

head.ce:title: Carbon nitride quantum dots control RuO2-TiO2/Ti coated anodes for degradation of high concentration phenol

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Complex Nonferrous Metal Resources Clean Utilization
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were fabricated by a one-step hydrothermal method, and RuO2-TiO2-N-CQDs/Ti composite-coated anodes were fabricated using a sol-gel method. The results indicate that the doping of N-CQDs generates many vacancy defects on the surface of the composite coating. As the content of N-CQDs increases, the width and depth of surface cracks on the coating gradually decrease, and the crystal size decreases. The doping of N-CQDs can effectively improve the electrochemical performance of the anode. When the doping amount of N-CQDs is 9.7 %, the surface charge capacity is increased by 243.5 %, the polarisation potential is decreased by 160 mV, and the self-corrosion potential is increased by 87.1 %. Compared to the traditional RuO2-TiO2/Ti anode, when the content of N-CQDs is increased to 9.7 %, the removal efficiency of phenol on the RuO2-TiO2-N-CQDs/Ti composite-coated electrode is increased by 93.2 %. In comparison, the removal efficiency of chemical oxygen demand within 1 h is increased by 48.7 %. In this study, we fully utilised the effective electron transfer and structural changes induced by N element doping to improve the electrocatalytic performance of the electrode, providing a new approach for mineralising high-concentration phenol in Ru/Ti electrodes.

ce:section.ce:para: Precious metal oxide-coated titanium electrodes (dimensionally stable anodes (DSAs)) have good electrocatalytic activity, stable size, and long service life. They have become important anode materials in the electrochemical industry and are widely used in chlor-alkali, electrolysis, electroplating, and batteries [1–5]. The electrode material is mainly composed of an active coating and an inert substrate [6]. As titanium belongs to valve-type metals, forming a stable passivation film on the surface is simple and has good corrosion resistance. Therefore, industrial electrode materials usually use titanium as a substrate, such as titanium-based Ru Ti binary oxide coatings, which have become a widely used electrode material [7]. However, the single Ru Ti binary coating has shortcomings, such as low catalytic efficiency and instability, which limit its development. Therefore, it is of interest to improve the catalytic performance and stability of the electrode [8]. One effective method is to introduce suitable carbon-based carriers, which can not only improve the conductivity of composite materials, but also effectively protect catalyst nanoparticles from corrosion and oxidation [9].

Among various carbon-based materials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a new type of nanocarbon with abundant surface functional groups and the ability to induce charge redistribution, have become important candidate materials to enhance the electrocatalytic performances of transition metal-based materials due to their inherent physical and chemical properties [10]. In addition, the rich functional groups on the surface of CQDs provide abundant anchoring and active sites for engineering multi-component high-performance composite materials. For example, Zhao et al. [11] prepared CQDs/SnO2-Co3O4 nanocomposites using hydrothermal methods. The introduction of CQDs enables the improvement of the conductivity of the composite material, promoting electron transfer between the catalyst active site and electronic surface, and thereby largely enhancing the electrocatalytic water oxidation performance. Yang et al. [12] fabricated Co and Fe-Co-doped CQDs (CoFe CQDs), and reported that the introduction of CQDs significantly increased the conductivity, accelerated the electron transfer in electrocatalytic reactions, and thus significantly improved the electrocatalytic OER activity and stability. The rich functional groups (-OH, -COOH, etc.) on the surface of CQDs can effectively coordinate with metal ions in empty d orbitals, generating stable CQDs metal ion coordination composite materials [13]. The introduced CQDs can restrict metal nanoparticles by forming ultrafine crystals with stable nanostructures, thereby preventing metal nanoparticle aggregation and further growth [14]. Therefore, combining nanoparticles with CQDs provides a promising approach for designing and developing composite materials based on CQDs with stable nanostructures. For example, Tang et al. [15] fabricated a novel composite material CQDs/NiFe layered double hydroxide (LDH) and reported that the strong electrostatic interaction between NiFe LDH and CQDs, as well as the generated C-O-Ni/Fe bonds, significantly enhanced the electrochemical activity of CQDs/NiFe LDH composite materials toward OER. In addition, CQDs doped with non-metallic elements (N, P, S, Cl) exhibit superior electrocatalytic performances.

Impurity atom-modified CQDs can control the morphology of metal nanoparticles, prevent an excessive aggregation of metal nanoparticles, and provide a larger electrochemical active surface area for electrocatalytic processes. The doping of impurity atoms in CQDs can change the surface charge distribution of the carbon layer and thereby significantly improve the conductivity of the material [16–19]. Tian et al. [14] prepared an N-CQDs MnO2 by a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. The introduction of N-CQDs not only improved the conductivity of the catalyst, but also effectively increased the specific surface area of MnO2. In addition, the inherent reducibility of CQDs is conducive to forming more Mn3+ in the catalyst during the synthesis process, further enhancing its OER activity and stability. There are a few reports on N-element-modified CQDs used in water treatment. In this study, N-CQDs were prepared using the hydrothermal method, and RuO2-TiO2-N-CQDs/Ti electrodes were fabricated using the sol-gel method for degrading organic wastewater containing phenol. The degradation rate of phenol and removal rate of chemical oxygen demand (COD) was measured using high-performance liquid chromatography and potassium permanganate titration, respectively.

The substances involved in this experiment include RuCl3·3H2O with a mass fraction of 37 %, butyl titanate (analytical grade), H2C2O4 (analytical grade), NaOH (analytical grade), HCl (36 %), n-butanol (analytical grade), citric acid (analytical grade), urea (analytical grade), and Na2SO4 (analytical grade). We also employed a 10 mm × 1 mm × 10 mm TA2 titanium plate.

Using a one-step hydrothermal method, we used citric acid as a carbon source and urea as a nitrogen source to prepare N-CQDs. We added 3 g of citric acid and 3 g of urea to 10 mL of distilled water. Then, we stirred at room temperature for 1 h and transferred to a 50-mL stainless-steel reactor lined with polytetrafluoroethylene. We then carried out a hydrothermal reaction at 180 °C for 8 h. After cooling the reactor to room temperature, we poured out the solution inside, centrifuged it at 10000 r/min for 30 m, and filtered it with a 0.22-μm organic nylon membrane. This yielded ae brown nitrogen-doped CQD solution. The diameter of the N-CQDs particles was measured using the software Nano Measurer.

Titanium plate pretreatment: We polished the titanium plate to increase the roughness and remove surface stains and oxide layers, and then washed it with a 10 % NaOH alkaline solution at 50 °C for 30 min and etched it with a 10 % oxalic acid solution at 80 °C for 120 min.

Preparation of a gel: We dissolved 3.5 g of citric acid into 25 mL of absolute ethanol, followed by heating and stirring at 60 °C for 3 h. We dissolved RuCl3 and butyl titanate in anhydrous ethanol in a ratio of n (Ru): n (Ti) = 3:7 and then dropped them into the above solution at a rate of 1 drop/s. We stirred at 60 °C to fully mix and evaporate the excess anhydrous ethanol. After cooling, we added the prepared N-CQDs solution, stirred it, and allowed it to stand for 24 h.

Coating sintering: We evenly brushed the prepared gel on the surface of the treated titanium plate with a fine brush, dried it in a 100 °C drying oven for 10 min, and transferred it to a 500 °C muffle furnace for roasting for 10 min. We then took it out and waited to cool before recoating, drying, and roasting it naturally. This was repeated at least 15 times until the coating liquid was used. Finally, we carried out sintering at 500 °C for 1 h. Fig. 1 shows a schematic of the RuO2-TiO2-N-CQDs/Ti anode synthesis.

The electrochemical testing was conducted using a Shanghai Chenhua CHI-660E electrochemical workstation with a three-electrode system. The working electrode was a RuO2-TiO2-N-CQDs/Ti electrode, with a testing area of 1 cm2. The auxiliary electrode was a 1 cm2 platinum plate electrode, while the reference electrode was mercuric oxide. The electrolyte was a saturated KCl solution. The testing was carried out at room temperature. The scanning rate for Tafel curve testing was 10 mV/s, with a voltage range of 0.1–1.3 V. The scanning rate for cyclic voltammetry (CV) curve testing was 50 mV/s, with a voltage range of 0.1–1.3 V. The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) scanning rate was 50 mV/s, while the voltage range was 0.1–1.5 V. The sensitivity was 0.001.

The prepared titanium anode was analysed using X-ray diffraction (XRD) (X Pert PROMPD) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha). The XRD test results were analysed and processed using the Jade 6.5 software. The surface morphologies of the titanium anodes were observed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (VEGA-3SBH) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Tecnai G2 TF30). The prepared N-CQDs were characterised using Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Bruker Tensor 27), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and XPS.

We used high-performance liquid chromatography (Waters 2996, USA) with a Waters RP18 (5) chromatographic column (3.9 mm × 150 mm), diode array detector, and flow rate of 1.00 mL · min-1. The mobile phase comprised 50 % water and 50 % methanol (v/v). The employed λ was 254 nm, while the column temperature was 35 °C. The potassium dichromate titration method was used to determine the COD.

We used citric acid as a carbon source and urea as a nitrogen source to prepare N-CQDs through a one-step hydrothermal method. The microstructure and morphology of the prepared N-CQDs were studied by FTIR spectroscopy, TEM, and XPS, as shown in Fig. 2 .

According to the FTIR spectrum analysis of N-CQDs in Fig. 2 (a), the peak at 3450 cm−1 is attributed to the N-H stretching vibration, the peak at 3350–3216 cm−1 is attributed to the C=N/-OH stretching vibration [20], the peak at 1677–1625 cm−1 is attributed to the C=C/C = 0 asymmetric stretching vibration, the peak at 1453 cm−1 is attributed to the C-N stretching vibration, the peak at 1156 cm−1 is attributed to the C-O/C-C stretching vibration, and the characteristic peak at 1070 cm−1 is attributed to the C-N stretching vibration of the primary aromatic amine. The peak at 578 cm−1 belongs to the out of plane bending vibration of C-H [21]. Fig. 2 (b) shows the XPS spectrum of N-CQDs prepared by the one-step hydrothermal method, which consists of C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s. According to the high-resolution spectrum of C 1s, there are peaks attributed to C=C/C-C, C-N, C-O, and C=N/C=O at 284.8, 286.1, 287.3, and 288.6 eV, respectively [22]. The high-resolution spectrum analysis of O 1s shows peaks attributed to C=O, C-O, C-O-C at 529.4, 531.4, and 532.9 eV, respectively [23]. The high-resolution spectrum analysis of N 1s shows peaks attributed to C-N, O=C-N/C-N-C, and N-H on primary aromatic amines at 397.6, 399.3, and 400.2 eV [24]. This is consistent with the FTIR spectroscopy results, which indicate that the prepared N-CQDs have oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface. Fig. 2 (f) shows a TEM image of N-CQDs. The shape of N-CQDs is approximately spherical; the N-CQDs are relatively dispersed, and there are no clusters. The particle diameters of N-CQDs are smaller than 10 nm. The average particle diameter is 6.25 nm. The energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) element mapping of N-CQDs in Fig. 2(g–k) reveals the presence of C, N, and O elements. The distribution of C and O is relatively uniform, while the N elements are mainly distributed near the CQDs. This indicates the successful preparation of N-CQDs in this experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterisation of the RuO2-TiO2-N-CQDs/Ti electrode
ce:section.ce:para: N-CQDs, RuO2, and TiO2, were deposited on the pretreated titanium plate surface by the sol-gel method to study the influence of N-CQDs doping on the electrode surface morphology, phase composition, and element valence state. A TEM analysis shows that, in the composite coating, N-CQDs have two types of crystal planes, (100) and (002), with crystal plane spacings of 0.21 and 0.32 nm, respectively (Fig. 3(a–d, e) ) [25]. RuO2 and TiO2 mainly have (110) crystal planes, with crystal plane spacings of 0.318 nm (corresponding to PDF card 40–1290) and 0.324 nm (corresponding to PDF card 21–1276) (Fig. 3(a–c)). According to the element mapping, the nitrogen elements are uniformly distributed. This indicates that N-CQDs are uniformly dispersed in the coating without aggregation. The SEM image shows that the micromorphology of the anode coating doped with N-CQDs is the same as that of doped CQDs with a mud crack morphology [26]. Its changing trend is also consistent with that of CQDs doping, and the width and depth of cracks are inversely proportional to the content of N-CQDs. (Fig. 3(f and g)). This may be attributed to combining functional groups at the edge of N-CQDs with coated metal oxides to form metal oxide nano complexes (∗N-Ru4+, ∗O-Ru4+, etc.). Moreover, the surface electroneutrality of CQDs by N-element doping promotes electron transfer, provides N-CQDs with more active sites, and thus makes the N-CQDs-based composites ideal materials for electrocatalysis [27].

An XRD analysis was performed to study the crystal structure of the synthetic material, as shown in Fig. 3 (h). The XRD analysis results show that the electrode coatings comprise rutile and anatase phases of TiO2 and RuO2. As shown in Fig. 3 (h), the rutile phase of TiO2 is more abundant than the anatase phase. With the addition of N-CQDs, the anatase phase peak at 25.4° disappears, the rutile phase peak at 27.6° increases, while the RuO2 phase peak near 35° does not change. Therefore, with N-CQDs, the TiO2 oxide changes from anatase to rutile. The crystal size began to decrease with the increase in the N-CQD content. The grain size of the electrode without N-CQDs was 17.5 nm. When the content of N-CQDs increased to 9.7 %, the grain size of the coating was 13.7 nm, which decreased by 21.7 %. The deficient structure and N-CQDs due to oxygen vacancies may hinder the increase in crystal size [28].

The XPS analysis showed that, with the incorporation of N-CQDs, O 1s of the RuO2-TiO2-N-CQDs/Ti electrode coating shifted negatively by 0.42–0.43 eV (Fig. 3 (i)). Moreover, the intensity of the peak of M − OH (M = Ru or Ti) near 529.54 eV in the hydrated surface layer increased (near 529.96 eV for the RuO2-TiO2/Ti electrode). The intensity of the M − O peak of the metal oxide crystal lattice near 531.61 eV decreased (near 532.04 eV for the RuO2-TiO2/Ti electrodes). This indicates that, with the doping of N-CQDs, the electrode coating transitioned from M − O to M-OH; i.e., the metal oxide was functionalised by hydroxyl groups [29–33]. The Ru 3d high-resolution spectrum shows that the peak at 280.5 eV is attributed to Ru 3d5/2 with low binding energy, which is generated by the oxidation state of Ru4+ [34,35], while that at 284.8 eV is attributed to Ru 3d3/2. Fig. 3 (j) shows that the 288.6 and 286.4 eV peaks are attributed to Ru3+ and Ru4+, respectively. Owing to various functional groups (nitrogen ∗N-Ru4+ and oxygen ∗O-Ru4+ functional groups) on the surface of N-CQDs [36], the single peak at 282 eV is decomposed into two peaks for a stable CQDs-metal ion coordination composite [37]. The Ru 3p high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 3 (k)) shows that the signals at 461.7 and 484.1 eV correspond to the chemical states of Ru 3p3/2 (Ru3+) and Ru 3p1/2 (Ru4+), respectively. In contrast, the peaks at 458.6 and 464.1 eV are attributed to the combination of Ru-Ti [38,39]. For the RuO2-TiO2-N-CQDs/Ti electrode, there is also a weak peak at 486.3 eV, which may be attributed to the combination of N-CQDs with RuO2 (i.e., Ru4+) [40]. The Ti 2p spectrum has a peak generated by Ti 2p3/2 at 458.6 eV, while the peak at 464.1 eV is attributed to Ti 2p1/2, which corresponds to the Ru 3p spectrum [31]. With the doping of N-CQDs, the electrode peak disappears at 457.9 eV, which may be due to changes in the electronic structure of the coating induced by nitrogen doping [41,42]. The XPS analysis of N 1s shows that the doping of N-CQDS yielded C-N and, C=N peaks with binding energies of 400 and 400.6 eV, respectively.

The electrocatalytic performances of anodes with different N-CQDs contents were studied. CV, polarisation (LSV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and Tafel curves were measured to characterise the electrochemical performances of the anodes. The effect of N-CQDs doping on the degradation of high-concentration phenol was studied using high-performance liquid chromatography.

Regarding the EIS curve, the size of the semi-arc diameter is commonly used to evaluate the charge transfer resistance between the electrode coating and substrate. A smaller diameter implies a smaller resistance and better electrocatalytic activity. As shown in Fig. 4 (a) , with the increase in the N-CQDs content, the radius of the EIS semicircle initially decreases and then increases. When the N-CQDs content is 9.7 %, the diameter of the EIS curve semicircle is the smallest, indicating the highest charge transfer efficiency between the electrode coating and substrate and electrocatalytic performance.

As shown in Fig. 4 (b), with the increase in the N-CQDs content, the area enclosed by the CV curve initially increases and then decreases. When the N-CQDs content is 9.7 %, the area enclosed by the CV curve is the largest. This indicates that the surface charge capacity of the electrode initially increases and then decreases with the increase in N-CQDs content. The surface charge capacity of the electrode reaches the maximum when the content of N-CQDs reaches 9.7 %. According to formula Q = SA/v (Q is the charge capacity, S is the CV curve area, and v is the scanning rate), when the doping amount of N-CQDs is 3.4 %, 6.7 %, and %9.7 %, the surface charge capacity is 3.1 %, 62.5 %, and 243.5 % higher than that of the undoped N-CQDs electrode, respectively. These results show that the doping of N-CQDs in the anode coating can largely improve the electrochemical performance of the electrode. This may be attributed to functional groups such as -OH, -CH2, and -NH2 on the surface of N-CQDs, which make N-CQDs stable on the electrode surface coating and thus improve the electrocatalytic performance of the electrode [43,44]. The doping of N elements can also induce effective electron transfer and electron structure change, and improve the electrocatalytic performance of the electrode. When multiple transition metals are co-doped, the modulation and synergies between the metals amplify this effect and further improve the electrocatalytic performance [45]. When the content of N-CQDs exceeds 9.7 %, the surface charge capacity of the electrode decreases because excessive N-CQDs reduce the number of surface cracks of the electrode and thus reduce the effective reaction area of the electrode.

As shown in Fig. 4 (c), with the increase in N-CQDs content, the polarisation potential of the electrode exhibited a trend of an initial decrease followed by an increase. When the content of N-CQDs reached 9.7 %, the polarisation potential of the electrode was lowest (1.36 V). This indicates that the electrocatalytic activity of the electrode is highest when the content of N-CQDs is 9.7 %. The doping of N-CQDs can improve the electrocatalytic activity of the electrode [46] owing to two factors. (1) N-CQDs, as a good carrier, can prevent the accumulation of metals or metal oxides and thus significantly improve the catalytic activity [47]. (2) Because N is more electronegative than C, the doping of heteroatoms (N) can induce effective charge transfer and structural distortion of neighbouring carbon atoms, change the local state density, adjust the electronic structure, and enhance the intrinsic activity of the active sites [48,49]. When the content of N-CQDs exceeds 9.7 %, the polarisation potential of the electrode begins to increase, which also occurs because the excess N-CQDs reduce the specific surface area of the electrode and further reduce the reaction area of the electrode.

Fig. 4 (d) shows the Tafel curves of RuO2-TiO2-N-CQDs/Ti anodes with different N-CQDs doping amounts. The self-corrosion potential (E
corr ) of the anode is inversely proportional to the N-CQDs content. This indicates that the addition of N-CQDs is beneficial to the corrosion resistance of the anode. Compared to the anode without N-CQDs, when the doped content of N-CQDs reached 3.4 %, 6.7 %, and 9.7 %, E
corr of the prepared anode was increased by 47.5 %, 74.2 %, and 87.1 %, respectively, as N-CQDs have good hydrophilicity and can be better attached to the electrode surface in the electrolyte, and thus largely improve the structural stability of RuO2-TiO2-N-CQDs/Ti in the electrochemical reaction process [50–52]. In addition, the presence of ∗N-Ru4+ and ∗O-Ru4+ can also improve the structural stability of the composite-coated electrode [53].

To study the long-term stability of the prepared electrode, a RuO2-TiO2-9.7 %(N-CQDs)/Ti anode was used to conduct several electrochemical cycles in a 1-mol/L H2SO4 solution. The results are shown in Fig. 4 (g). With the increase in the number of CV cycles, the area enclosed by the CV curve of the electrode gradually decreases; i.e., the electrocatalytic activity of the electrode gradually decreases. After 70 cycles, the electrocatalytic activity of the electrode decreased significantly. However, its electrocatalytic activity did not disappear (there was certain electrocatalytic activity), which indicates that the electrode has good long-term stability.

Fig. 4 (e) and (f) show that the degradation rates of phenol and COD are strongly related to the content of N-CQDs. When the content of N-CQDs reaches 3.4 %, 6.7 %, and 9.7 %, phenol degradation rates within 1 h are increased by 65.6 %, 70.4 %, and 93.2 %, respectively, compared to that without doping. The degradation rates of COD are increased by 28.2 %, 38.5 %, and 48.7 %, respectively, because N-CQDs can increase the electron transfer rate, which leads to an increase in the production rate of ∙OH, and further increases the formation rate of the strong metal oxidiser MOx(∙OH), which is the main substance that directly degrades phenol. An increase in the formation rate of MOx(∙OH) leads to an increase in the degradation rate of phenol [54] (Fig. 5 ). Moreover, the doped heteroatoms (N) may induce effective charge transfer and structural distortion of adjacent carbon atoms (because N is more electronegative than C), regulate the electronic structure, enhance the intrinsic activity of the active site, and promote the adsorption and desorption of intermediates. As shown in Fig. 4 (e) and (f), owing to the existence of degradation intermediates, the degradation rate of COD is considerably lower than that of phenol. When the N-CQDs doping content is 9.7 %, the degradation rate of phenol is 89 %, while the degradation rate of COD is only 58.3 %. When the content of N-CQDs exceeds 9.7 %, the degradation rates of phenol and COD begin to decrease because the increase in the content of N-CQDs reduces the crack width and depth on the electrode surface, reduces the contact area between organic matter and electrode, and leads to the decrease in the degradation rate.

To study the effect of N-CQDs doping on the performances of RuO2-TiO2/Ti-coated electrodes, we used the one-step hydrothermal method to prepare N-CQDs, which was also combined with the sol-gel method to fabricate RuO2-TiO2-N-CQDs/Ti composite-coated electrodes. In addition, we studied the degradation of a phenol-containing solution. The findings of this study can be summarised as follows.
(1)
SEM and TEM analysed the surface morphology of the composite coating electrode. N-CQDs had (100) and (002) crystal faces with interfacial spacings of 0.21 and 0.32 nm, respectively. RuO2 and TiO2 had mainly (110) crystal faces with interfacial spacings of 0.318 and 0.324 nm, respectively. XPS was used to analyse the valence states of the surface oxides of the composite coatings. With the doping of N-CQDs, the electrode coatings changed from M − O to M-OH. With the increase in the N-CQDs content, the crystal size decreased. When the content of N-CQDs increased to 9.7 %, the grain size of the coating was 13.7 nm.
(2)
The electrochemical performances of the composite-coated electrode were investigated by CV, polarisation, and Tafel curve measurements. The N-CQDs doping could effectively improve the electrochemical performance of the composite-coated electrode. When the N-CQDs doping amount was 9.7 %, the surface charge capacity was increased by 243.5 %. At the same time, the polarisation potential was reduced by 160 mV. The self-corrosion potential was increased by 87.1 %.
(3)
Compared to the traditional RuO2-TiO2/Ti anode, when the content of N-CQDs increased to 9.7 %, the removal efficiencies of phenol and COD for the RuO2-TiO2-N-CQDs/Ti composite-coated electrode at 1 h were increased by 93.2 % and 48.7 %, respectively.
Zhengyi Li: Writing – original draft. Zhaohui Han: Project administration. Yang Xu: Data curation. Sikang Wei: Data curation. Lei Xu: Software. Shenggang Zhou: Visualization.
The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings cannot be shared at this time as the data also forms part of an ongoing study.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51864030); the China Petrochemical Corporation (No. 224110); the National Key R&D Program of China (Nos. 2018YFC1901904); the Yunnan Basic Research Key Project (Nos. 202101AS070023, Nos. 202401AT070405); the Yunnan Provincial Science and Technology Talents Program (No. 2019HB003); the Yunnan High-Level Talent Training Support Plan.
